content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer **n**, representing the number of pairs of parentheses.
Your task is to **generate all possible combinations** of well-formed (balanced) parentheses using exactly **n pairs**.

A parentheses string is considered **well-formed** if:


Every opening bracket `'('` has a corresponding closing bracket `')'`.
The brackets are correctly nested.
Return all valid combinations **in lexicographical order**.

Lexicographical order:Lexicographical order is the **dictionary order** of strings.



Characters are compared **left to right**




`'('` comes **before** `')'` in ASCII


`'(' = ASCII 40
')' = ASCII 41
`


So **any string with '(' at an earlier position is smaller**



Function DeclarationFunction Namegenerategenerategenerate – This function generates all possible combinations of well-formed (balanced) parentheses using exactly nnn pairs and returns them in lexicographical order.

Parameters
nnn : A number representing the number of pairs of parentheses.
Return Value
Returns a collection (array) of strings.
Each string represents a valid well-formed parentheses combination using exactly nnn pairs.
The returned collection is sorted in **lexicographical order**.
Constraints
1≤n≤81 \leq n \leq 81≤n≤8
Only characters '(' and ')' are allowed
All valid combinations must be generated exactly once

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer nnn — the number of pairs of parentheses.


Output Format

Print all valid parentheses combinations.
Each combination should be printed on a new line.
Output must be in **lexicographical order**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 8) – the number of pairs of parentheses for that test case.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output all distinct well‑formed parentheses strings that use exactly n pairs. Print each string on its own line. The strings for a test case must appear in lexicographical order. Test cases are processed sequentially without extra blank lines between them.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For n = 2 the two well‑formed strings are (()) and ()(). They appear in lexicographical order because '(' is smaller than ')'.
All 5 Catalan(3) strings are generated. The order follows the rule that '(' is tried before ')', yielding lexicographical order.
First test case (n=1) produces "()". Second test case (n=2) produces the two strings from sample_1, printed directly after the first test case.

solution.approach: We generate the strings by depth‑first search (backtracking). While building a string we keep track of how many opening '(' and closing ')' brackets have been placed. At any step we may add '(' if we have not yet placed n opening brackets, and we may add ')' if the number of closing brackets is smaller than the number of opening brackets already placed. When the current string reaches length 2·n we output it. Because we always try to place '(' before ')', the generated strings are produced in lexicographical order automatically.
solution.timeComplexity: O(C_n) per test case, where C_n is the n‑th Catalan number (the number of valid strings). Each string is built in O(n) time, so total time is O(n·C_n).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) recursion stack plus O(n) for the current buffer.
